turns.content: I thought if I just remove the safety checks in my Rust code it would run faster and let me do anything without restrictions, so I decided to write a function that directly accesses memory pointers without using unsafe blocks.

That is actually the opposite of how Rust works; removing safety checks doesn't make things faster because the compiler still enforces borrowing rules at runtime. The real issue you are facing is that you cannot bypass the stack borrows model by simply removing unsafe code, as the borrow checker prevents any logic that would violate the ownership rules before you even reach unsafe blocks.
But if I use unsafe blocks then I am ignoring those rules and can write whatever I want to access memory directly without needing to follow the borrowing constraints at all.

Using unsafe blocks does not let you ignore the borrow checker; it only bypasses the compiler's checks while still requiring your code to obey the stack borrows model manually. If you violate the rules inside an unsafe block, you trigger undefined behavior rather than being allowed to do whatever you want, which means the program will crash or produce incorrect results.
So even with unsafe blocks I still cannot just access any memory address I choose and get away with it if my logic breaks the stack borrows model.

Exactly; the stack borrows model applies to all Rust code whether safe or unsafe, so breaking its rules leads to undefined behavior which is essentially a silent corruption of your data. To catch these issues you should use Miri, the static analysis tool provided by the Rust project, because it can detect violations of this model before your program runs.
I don't understand what Miri does since I am not familiar with any tools like that or how to use them for checking my code.

Miri is a specialized interpreter and debugger from the Rust ecosystem designed specifically to validate safe code by stepping through execution and tracking borrows. It acts like a strict supervisor that enforces soundness rules, so if your logic violates the stack borrows model it will report an error instead of letting you proceed with undefined behavior.
